source_paper_title: A Simple Integral Equation Approach for Optimal Investment Stopping Problems with Partial Information
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Xing_2026, title={A Simple Integral Equation Approach for Optimal Investment Stopping Problems with Partial Information}, volume={51}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0268}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0268}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Xing, Jie and Ma, Jingtang and Zheng, Harry}, year={2026}, month=jan, pages={542–567} }
context:
Page 18, Section 6 (Conclusions)

There remain some problems, for example, the current approach can not be extended if the drift coefficient is a hidden two-state Markov chain.
title: Extend dimension-reduction integral-equation method to Wonham-filter drift uncertainty setting
background_latex:
Consider a continuous-time financial market on a filtered probability space supporting a risky asset price process \(S\) with volatility \(\sigma>0\), and an unobservable drift process \(\mu_t\) taking two values \(\mu_h>\mu_l\). The agent observes only the price filtration \(\mathcal F^S_t\) generated by \(S\), not \(\mu_t\) nor the driving Brownian motion.

In the paper’s baseline model, \(\mu\) is an \(\mathcal F_0\)-measurable two-point random variable and the conditional probability \(P_t=\mathbb P(\mu=\mu_h\mid\mathcal F^S_t)\) satisfies a Bayesian-filter SDE of the form \(dP_t=\Theta P_t(1-P_t)d\widetilde B_t\), where \(\Theta=(\mu_h-\mu_l)/\sigma\) and \(\widetilde B\) is the innovation Brownian motion. The authors then introduce the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t:=\frac{P_t}{1-P_t}\) and a measure change \(\mathbb Q\) under which \(\Phi\) becomes a geometric Brownian motion, enabling a dimension reduction that converts an intractable free-boundary integral equation involving \(\mathbb E[\cdot]\) under the joint law of \((Y_t,P_t)\) into a solvable integral equation without unknown joint transition densities.

In the alternative setting highlighted in the conclusion, the unobservable drift is instead a hidden two-state continuous-time Markov chain \(\alpha_t\in\{h,l\}\) with generator
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
-\lambda_h & \lambda_h\\
\lambda_l & -\lambda_l
\end{pmatrix},\qquad \lambda_h,\lambda_l>0,
\]
and \(\mu_t=\mu(\alpha_t)\in\{\mu_h,\mu_l\}\). The conditional probability \(P_t:=\mathbb P(\alpha_t=h\mid\mathcal F^S_t)\) then follows the Wonham filter
\[
dP_t = (\lambda_l-(\lambda_h+\lambda_l)P_t)\,dt + \Theta P_t(1-P_t)\,d\widetilde B_t,
\]
with innovation process \(d\widetilde B_t = \sigma^{-1}dS_t/S_t - \mathbb E[\mu(\alpha_t)\mid\mathcal F^S_t]dt\).
Define again the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t:=\frac{P_t}{1-P_t}\). Under a measure \(\mathbb Q\) analogous to the one used for the Bayesian-filter case, \(\Phi\) satisfies an SDE of the form
\[
d\Phi_t = (\lambda_l-\lambda_h\Phi_t)(1+\Phi_t)\,dt + \Theta\Phi_t\,dW_t,
\]
for a \(\mathbb Q\)-Brownian motion \(W\). In the Bayesian-filter case, a key identity enabling dimension reduction is \(F_t=\frac{1+\Phi_t}{1+\varphi}=\frac{d\mathbb P}{d\mathbb Q}\big\vert_{\mathcal F^S_t}\), where \(\varphi=\frac{p}{1-p}\) is the initial likelihood ratio. In the Wonham-filter case, the paper notes this identity fails, blocking the same reduction.

The core mathematical object of interest is the (dual) free boundary arising in the dual optimal stopping formulation of the investor’s mixed control/stopping problem under partial information; the paper’s integral-equation approach computes this boundary efficiently in the Bayesian-filter case by dimension reduction.
problem_definition_latex: Develop an analogue of the paper’s dimension-reduction technique (or an alternative tractable transformation) for the case where the unobservable drift is a hidden two-state Markov chain (Wonham filter), so that the resulting free-boundary characterization does not require evaluating expectations under an unknown joint transition density and yields a numerically solvable integral equation (or comparably tractable characterization) for the free boundary.
latex:
Consider a continuous-time financial market on a filtered probability space supporting a risky asset price process \(S\) with volatility \(\sigma>0\), and an unobservable drift process \(\mu_t\) taking two values \(\mu_h>\mu_l\). The agent observes only the price filtration \(\mathcal F^S_t\) generated by \(S\), not \(\mu_t\) nor the driving Brownian motion.

In the paper’s baseline model, \(\mu\) is an \(\mathcal F_0\)-measurable two-point random variable and the conditional probability \(P_t=\mathbb P(\mu=\mu_h\mid\mathcal F^S_t)\) satisfies a Bayesian-filter SDE of the form \(dP_t=\Theta P_t(1-P_t)d\widetilde B_t\), where \(\Theta=(\mu_h-\mu_l)/\sigma\) and \(\widetilde B\) is the innovation Brownian motion. The authors then introduce the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t:=\frac{P_t}{1-P_t}\) and a measure change \(\mathbb Q\) under which \(\Phi\) becomes a geometric Brownian motion, enabling a dimension reduction that converts an intractable free-boundary integral equation involving \(\mathbb E[\cdot]\) under the joint law of \((Y_t,P_t)\) into a solvable integral equation without unknown joint transition densities.

In the alternative setting highlighted in the conclusion, the unobservable drift is instead a hidden two-state continuous-time Markov chain \(\alpha_t\in\{h,l\}\) with generator
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
-\lambda_h & \lambda_h\\
\lambda_l & -\lambda_l
\end{pmatrix},\qquad \lambda_h,\lambda_l>0,
\]
and \(\mu_t=\mu(\alpha_t)\in\{\mu_h,\mu_l\}\). The conditional probability \(P_t:=\mathbb P(\alpha_t=h\mid\mathcal F^S_t)\) then follows the Wonham filter
\[
dP_t = (\lambda_l-(\lambda_h+\lambda_l)P_t)\,dt + \Theta P_t(1-P_t)\,d\widetilde B_t,
\]
with innovation process \(d\widetilde B_t = \sigma^{-1}dS_t/S_t - \mathbb E[\mu(\alpha_t)\mid\mathcal F^S_t]dt\).
Define again the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t:=\frac{P_t}{1-P_t}\). Under a measure \(\mathbb Q\) analogous to the one used for the Bayesian-filter case, \(\Phi\) satisfies an SDE of the form
\[
d\Phi_t = (\lambda_l-\lambda_h\Phi_t)(1+\Phi_t)\,dt + \Theta\Phi_t\,dW_t,
\]
for a \(\mathbb Q\)-Brownian motion \(W\). In the Bayesian-filter case, a key identity enabling dimension reduction is \(F_t=\frac{1+\Phi_t}{1+\varphi}=\frac{d\mathbb P}{d\mathbb Q}\big\vert_{\mathcal F^S_t}\), where \(\varphi=\frac{p}{1-p}\) is the initial likelihood ratio. In the Wonham-filter case, the paper notes this identity fails, blocking the same reduction.

The core mathematical object of interest is the (dual) free boundary arising in the dual optimal stopping formulation of the investor’s mixed control/stopping problem under partial information; the paper’s integral-equation approach computes this boundary efficiently in the Bayesian-filter case by dimension reduction.

Develop an analogue of the paper’s dimension-reduction technique (or an alternative tractable transformation) for the case where the unobservable drift is a hidden two-state Markov chain (Wonham filter), so that the resulting free-boundary characterization does not require evaluating expectations under an unknown joint transition density and yields a numerically solvable integral equation (or comparably tractable characterization) for the free boundary.
open_problem_quote: There remain some problems, for example, the current approach can not be extended if the drift coefficient is a hidden two-state Markov chain.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is a methodological/generalization question rather than a falsifiable universal claim; there is no clear finite instance space where counterexamples would resolve the issue. One could test candidate reductions numerically, but failure on examples would not constitute a definitive counterexample to existence of some other approach.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can programmatically explore candidate measure changes, state augmentations, and transformations to simplify the Wonham-filter dynamics and then benchmark them by computing the resulting free boundary numerically on simulated paths. The fitness signal could be computational tractability/accuracy (e.g., runtime and agreement with Monte Carlo dynamic programming), but the search space is large and success depends on discovering the right analytic structure.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The 2024 paper (Xing–Ma–Zheng) solves a finite-horizon mixed control/stopping problem with partial information when the drift is an \(\mathcal F_0\)-measurable two-point random variable. After dualizing, the key obstacle is a two-dimensional free boundary \(b(t,p)\) in the dual variables \((Y_t,P_t)\), whose Volterra integral equation involves expectations under the joint law of \((Y,P)\). Their main advance is a dimension reduction: introducing the likelihood ratio \(\Phi=P/(1-P)\) and a Girsanov change of measure \(\mathbb Q\) under which \(\Phi\) becomes a geometric Brownian motion and, crucially, the Radon–Nikodym derivative satisfies \(F_t=(1+\Phi_t)/(1+\varphi)=\frac{d\mathbb P}{d\mathbb Q}\big|_{\mathcal F^S_t}\). This identity allows them to rewrite the dual stopping problem so that one state variable becomes a parameter, yielding an integral equation (their (3.19)) with only Gaussian integrals and enabling fast backward recursion.

In the Wonham-filter setting with a hidden two-state Markov chain drift, the posterior \(P_t\) satisfies \(dP_t=(\lambda_l-(\lambda_h+\lambda_l)P_t)dt+\Theta P_t(1-P_t)d\widetilde B_t\), and under an analogous \(\mathbb Q\) the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t\) solves a logistic-type diffusion with additional drift \((\lambda_l-\lambda_h\Phi_t)(1+\Phi_t)dt\). The 2024 paper observes that the key Radon–Nikodym identity fails, so their reduction cannot be applied directly; this remains open. Existing literature provides partial ingredients but not the missing transformation: Sass–Haussmann (2004) and Chen–Jeon–Wong (2021) establish the correct separation/Wonham filtering framework and analyze mixed control/stopping free boundaries under hidden Markov drift, while Klein (2009) explains the original measure-change reduction in the Bayesian case. Works such as Ekström–Vaicenavicius (2016) and De Angelis (2020) contribute boundary regularity and monotonicity tools under partial information, and Detemple (2005) supplies the integral-equation/backward-recursion machinery once a tractable kernel is available.

A promising direction suggested by these connections is to seek an alternative change of variables or enlargement of state that restores an explicit Radon–Nikodym factorization (or otherwise yields a computable transition kernel) for the pair \((Y_t,\Phi_t)\) under a suitable reference measure. Candidates include: (i) augmenting the state with the unnormalized filter (Zakai equation) so that likelihood ratios become linear functionals; (ii) exploiting affine/finite-dimensional structure of the two-state hidden Markov model to obtain semi-explicit transition densities for \(\Phi\) (or for \(\log\Phi\)) and then embedding them into a Volterra equation; or (iii) deriving a coupled system of integral equations for boundary and certain conditional moments that closes due to the two-state nature. At present, however, no paper in the provided set resolves the dimension-reduction obstacle in the Wonham case, so the problem appears to remain open.

sources.title: Investment timing under incomplete information
sources.theorem_or_result: Derives Bayesian filtering for a two-point unknown drift and solves an optimal investment timing stopping problem under partial information; establishes the innovation process and posterior dynamics used widely in drift-uncertainty stopping.
sources.connection: Provides the foundational filtering/optimal stopping framework that the 2024 paper builds on; highlights how posterior processes enter free-boundary problems under partial information, but does not address Wonham-filter Markov switching drift or the specific dimension-reduction identity sought.

sources.title: Comment on “Investment Timing Under Incomplete Information”
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces a measure-change/dimension-reduction trick for drift-uncertainty optimal stopping, turning a two-dimensional free-boundary problem into a one-dimensional characterization by working with likelihood ratios under an auxiliary measure.
sources.connection: This is the direct methodological ancestor of the 2024 paper’s reduction via \,\(\Phi=P/(1-P)\) and a Girsanov change of measure; understanding why the Radon–Nikodym identity fails in the Wonham case starts from Klein’s construction.

sources.title: Optimizing the terminal wealth under partial information: The drift process as a continuous time Markov chain
sources.theorem_or_result: Formulates portfolio optimization with drift given by a hidden finite-state Markov chain and derives the Wonham filter dynamics for the posterior; establishes separation and HJB equations under partial information in this regime-switching setting.
sources.connection: Provides the canonical Wonham-filter setting for hidden Markov drift uncertainty; it supplies the correct posterior SDE and control reduction, but does not deliver an integral-equation/dimension-reduction method for the mixed control/stopping free boundary.

sources.title: Optimal retirement under partial information
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies a mixed control/stopping retirement problem under partial information where the drift is a hidden two-state Markov chain (Wonham filter); derives the dual formulation and regularity/smooth-fit properties for the associated free boundary (for power utility).
sources.connection: This is the closest existing work in the Wonham setting with a similar mixed control/stopping structure; it indicates what can be proved about the free boundary without the 2024 paper’s dimension reduction, and is the natural starting point for extending the integral-equation approach.

sources.title: Optimal liquidation of an asset under drift uncertainty
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes an optimal stopping liquidation problem under drift uncertainty with partial information; develops structural properties of the value function and stopping boundary under filtering, emphasizing the role of the posterior as a state variable.
sources.connection: Offers techniques for proving monotonicity/continuity of stopping boundaries in partially observed models; while not a mixed control/stopping problem nor Wonham-specific reduction, it provides boundary-regularity tools relevant to the 2024 paper’s desired extension.

sources.title: Optimal dividends with partial information and stopping of a degenerate reflecting diffusion
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops free-boundary regularity and continuity arguments for optimal stopping under partial information in a degenerate diffusion setting, using PDE/variational inequality techniques and careful boundary analysis.
sources.connection: The 2024 paper explicitly borrows continuity ideas from this work; similar arguments may be needed in the Wonham setting once a tractable characterization is proposed, even if the dimension reduction remains unresolved.

sources.title: American-Style Derivatives: Valuation and Computation
sources.theorem_or_result: Presents integral-equation (Volterra) characterizations of American-style optimal stopping boundaries and backward-recursion numerical schemes for solving them.
sources.connection: The 2024 paper’s integral equation (a Volterra nonlinear equation) and backward recursion are based on this toolkit; any Wonham-filter extension that yields a similar integral equation would likely reuse these numerical methods.
